name: Alto Rey Loop from Bustares
name_original: Zarzuela de Jadraque desde Bustares — circular por el río Riatillo de la Vega
description: Tackle the difficult 20.4-mile Alto Rey Loop from Bustares, a challenging touring cycling route with 1983 feet of elevation gain and spectac
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Bustares are situated within the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, offering diverse landscapes for exploration. The region features mountainous terrain, including the prominent Alto Rey Peak, and tranquil river valleys like the Pelagallinas River. Cyclists can expect varied terrain, from paved roads to unpaved sections, traversing humid mountain forests and coniferous and oak woodlands. Best touring cycling routes around Bustares The most popular touring cycling route is Alto Rey from Hiendelaencina — circular by the Embalse de Alcorlo, a 39.4 miles (63.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route incorporates the Alto Rey Peak and views of the Embalse de Alcorlo. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Alto Rey Loop from Bustares, a difficult 20.4 miles (32.8 km) path. This route offers a challenging loop around Alto Rey, starting and ending in Bustares. Local touring cyclists also love the View of Alto Rey – Bustares loop from Las Navas de Jadraque, a 21.4 miles (34.4 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 20 minutes. Touring cycling around Bustares is defined by mountainous terrain, the Alto Rey Peak, and quiet river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, though many routes present significant elevation gains. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 20 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Bustares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Bustares?
A: There are 6 touring cycling routes available around Bustares, all of which are categorized as difficult due to the mountainous terrain and significant elevation gains.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Bustares?
A: The routes around Bustares feature varied terrain, ranging from paved roads to unpaved sections and even some singletrack. You'll traverse humid mountain forests, coniferous, and oak woodlands, with the prominent Alto Rey Peak providing a challenging backdrop.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: You can enjoy extraordinary landscapes and natural features. Notable sights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4187789" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey Peak (1,858 m)&lt;/a&gt;, offering spectacular views, and the tranquil Pelagallinas River. Other points of interest include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5440944" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Pelagallinas River&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6716397" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sombrero Rock&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6716456" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Arroyo de La Sandría&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region offers cultural points of interest. The nearby village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4681084" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hiendelaencina&lt;/a&gt;, known for its 19th-century silver mining, features the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6362851" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Museum of Silver and Mining of Hiendelaencina&lt;/a&gt; and remnants of its industrial heritage.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes starting from Bustares?
A: Yes, a popular circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1188043114/alto-rey-loop-from-bustares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey Loop from Bustares&lt;/a&gt;. This challenging 32.8 km path offers a loop around Alto Rey, starting and ending directly in Bustares.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Bustares?
A: The Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, where Bustares is located, offers diverse conditions. While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, the region's 'Nature in Vena' suggests it's generally appealing for outdoor activities. Given the cool waters of the Pelagallinas River in summer, and the mountainous terrain, spring and autumn might offer the most comfortable temperatures for cycling, avoiding extreme heat or potential winter conditions at higher elevations.

Q: Are there options for parking when starting a touring cycling route in Bustares?
A: While specific parking facilities aren't detailed, Bustares is a village within a natural park area. It's generally advisable to look for designated parking areas within the village or at common trailheads. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: Is public transport available to reach Bustares for cyclists?
A: Information on specific public transport options for cyclists to Bustares is limited. It's recommended to check regional bus services or consider driving to the area, especially if you're bringing your own touring bicycle.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Bustares?
A: All touring cycling routes currently listed around Bustares are categorized as difficult, featuring significant elevation gains and challenging terrain. There are no easy or moderate routes specifically for beginners in this selection.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay overnight in Bustares or nearby villages?
A: Bustares and its surrounding villages offer a tranquil setting, suggesting that local amenities like cafes, pubs, or accommodation might be available. For specific options, it's best to check local listings for Bustares village and nearby settlements like Hiendelaencina.

Q: Are the touring cycling routes around Bustares suitable for families?
A: Given that all listed touring cycling routes around Bustares are rated as difficult with significant elevation gains, they are generally not recommended for families with young children or those seeking very easy, flat rides. These routes require good to very good physical fitness.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the touring cycling routes in Bustares?
A: While the region is rich in natural beauty, specific regulations regarding dogs on cycling routes are not provided. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash, especially in natural park areas, and ensure they are well-behaved and can handle the challenging terrain and distances of these difficult routes.

Q: What are some of the longer touring cycling routes available?
A: One of the longest routes is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142595" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hiendelaencina Mining Heritage – Galve de Sorbe Castle loop from Hiendelaencina&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 97.7 km with over 1600 meters of elevation gain. Another substantial option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1185749905/alto-rey-from-hiendelaencina-circular-by-the-embalse-de-alcorlo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey from Hiendelaencina — circular by the Embalse de Alcorlo&lt;/a&gt;, covering 63.5 km.
tips: Small stone bridge over the Riatillo de la Vega stream.
Beautiful mountain village and starting point of the Camino Viejo del Alto Rey.

faqs.question: Is the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares suitable for touring cycling?
faqs.answer: While listed as a touring cycling route, the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares is generally considered more suitable for mountain biking or strenuous hiking. The trail features varied and often demanding terrain, including unpaved, natural surfaces, and a particularly steep, rocky section that may require pushing your bike. Traditional touring bicycles might find it very challenging.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied and challenging. You'll encounter sections of unpaved and natural paths, as well as some asphalt and paved roads. A notable feature is a quite steep section with rocks that demands careful navigation. The ascent to Alto Rey can involve gradients reaching 7-8%, and even over 10% after the deviation to the summit.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Alto Rey Loop?
faqs.answer: The route offers spectacular views, especially from the summit of Alto Rey. Key points of interest include the historic Slate Bridge over the Riatillo River and the charming village of Bustares. At the 1,858-meter summit of Alto Rey, you'll find the hermitage of Santo Alto Rey, a significant landmark and destination for a religious procession.

faqs.question: Where can I park when visiting the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the village of Bustares, which serves as the starting point for this loop. Specific parking areas for cyclists or hikers are usually available within or on the outskirts of the village.

faqs.question: Is the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on this trail. However, as the route passes through the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety on challenging terrain.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Alto Rey Loop?
faqs.answer: The best time to visit is typically during the spring or autumn months when temperatures are milder and the natural scenery is at its most vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters may bring snow and ice, making the already challenging terrain even more difficult or impassable.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares. The trail is located within the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara, which is freely accessible to the public. However, always respect local regulations and environmental guidelines.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route utilizes sections of several official trails. You will encounter parts of the [CIMA GU03] Alto Rey * Villares de Jadraque, [CIMA GU03] Alto Rey * Las Navas de Jadraque, Camino de El Ordial, Camino de Gascueña, and Camino de El Arroyo de las Fraguas.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete the Alto Rey Loop from Bustares?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 32.8 km loop is approximately 2 hours and 15 minutes. However, given its 'difficult' rating and challenging terrain, many cyclists, especially those on mountain bikes or those who need to push their bikes on steep sections, may take considerably longer. Plan for extra time, especially if you want to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this challenging route?
faqs.answer: Given the demanding nature of the Alto Rey Loop, it's essential to pack adequately. Bring plenty of water, high-energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike (if cycling), appropriate footwear, layers of clothing for changing weather, sun protection, and a map or GPS device. A helmet is crucial for cyclists.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the Alto Rey Loop?
faqs.answer: The village of Bustares, where the loop starts and ends, typically offers a few local establishments where you can find refreshments, cafes, or traditional Spanish meals. It's advisable to check their operating hours, especially outside of peak season.
